titleStmt.title: Letter from Fr William Keary to Fr Provincial Thomas V. Nolan, 19 November 1918
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: W. Keary

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Fr William Keary (1881-1958) to Fr Provincial Thomas V. Nolan (1867-1941) regarding the arrangement of Fr John Fitzgibbon's bills, and concerns about the result of the war.Fr. Thomas V Nolan was the Provincial of the Irish Province of the Society of Jesus (1912-1922) and was a member of the Distribution Committee which looked after the welfare and distribution of the Belgian refugees who arrived in Ireland as a consequence of the First World War.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Monday 19-11-18 Dear Fr. Provincial P.C. I have settled all those
bills &c of Fr. Fitzgibbon's, and all
are quite satisfied. There is no money owing either
to you or to me as resulting from
these bills. But I had to pay
other little debts due to his
groom and servant whom he
had not paid for two months
before his death. This amounts
'in toto' to Â£1 only. You will please excuse my
long delay in settling these little
things; but I am sure you realize
what long delays are often caused
owing to very frequent moves
and to the consequent disorgon-
ization of the mail I am now filling Fr. Fitzgibbons
office of Senior Chaplain (R.C.),
and I need scarcely tell you
how greatly I feel the need
of all the prayers and momentos
I can get. We are on the march into
Germany; and of course it is
an immense relief to know that
the poor 'boys' are out of danger
from shot and shell at long
last. I am much afraid of the
moral effects of victory on a
people who are on the whole
so hopelessly unaided to meet
with full christian self-control.
Good bye now dear Reverend Father, and please do
let me know if there is anything else I can do
for you in any way. Your's obediently in Xt. W. Keary S.J.
17th Field Ambulance
B.E.F.
